speaker: interviewer
text: This is Phyllis Dreazen speaking with Allen Caine in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. It's October 23, 1995. The language of the interview is English. What is your name, please? And spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Allen Caine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell your last name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: C-A-I-N-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cichowicz, C-I-C-H-O-W-I-C-Z, Ancik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your first name not Allen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Ancik.

speaker: interviewer
text: That--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they called me every place.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Call it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name on your birth certificate or formally?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- they're the same thing. As a matter of fact, I never had a birth certificate. It got lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names Israel and Anchel? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a name that you use in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell 'Ancik' for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-N-C-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland-- Ciechanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I'd have to look it up. [LAUGHS] C-I-E-C-H-O-A-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] Tell us, to begin, a little bit about your family and the place you grew up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in-- lived in that town, nine of us-- father and mother and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four-- seven kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before, when I was born, we were only four kids. My father-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was the-- my father's first marriage. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I was three months old, my mother died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the youngest from the four brothers. My father remarried. And the first-- from his second wife, he had two daughters and one son. They were much younger than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all live together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my grandparents to make it easier for my father. And the rest of them lived in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: To make it easier for him in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In every way-- wasn't enough food. To get rid of one mouth-- it was a big thing. And there was not even enough room to sleep at night. It was only-- like I mentioned, it was only one room-- kitchen, everything. It was one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my grandparents, it was a little easier. They had two rooms. I was always with my grandfather. I slept with my grandfather. I ate with my grandfather. Dinner, I used to sit on his lap and eat [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] till I was-- from three months till I was 15, till they took me away to a prison camp, till the end of my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your older brothers live?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older brothers lived with the-- their father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They all lived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your grandparents live in relation to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- it was a small town. It wasn't far away from each other. Sometimes I wouldn't see my father for weeks and weeks. In this way, I, I became a stranger wherever-- they came to see my father. We were raised-- I had no feelings for my brothers. And they didn't have any feelings for me. We were like strangers.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very sad.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is very sad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sometimes I used to bring my toys to play with my brothers. They took them away from me. I started to cry. So my father grabbed me-- just go to your grandparents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have a nice life. It was a bad life for me in every ways. I never had anybody my age to play with. According to my grandfather, he wanted me to grow up to be a rabbi. I almost succeeded until the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your mother's father or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was my mother's father and my stepmother's father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were two good people. Also, they-- my grandparents couldn't make a living for themself. They were already on retirement. So they had two daughters and a-- and one son. They supported their grandparents. So they supported me, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life was-- wasn't nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say their names as part of the record?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest brother's name was Faigel. The second brother's name was Zelek. And then it went Chaim, and my name, Anchel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my-- from my second mom, which were the kids which I-- I loved the kids very much. The oldest one was [NON-ENGLISH], Hadassah, like, and then Avrom, and then Bayla.

speaker: interviewer
text: The names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Aron Yitzchak. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother's name-- my deceased mother's name was Sara Rifke.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandparents' name was Aron David and Rochel.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second mother, how was she to you when you would see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] She didn't like my attitude because I never called her 'Ma.' Being a kid, I could not make up my mind. I didn't have the gut to call her 'Mom' because I knew she wasn't my mom. So she didn't like that idea. Her name was Toba. So she didn't care much for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But life wasn't so sweet between my father and my-- and the second wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't live in peace-- always arguments, fights. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't care much for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had it been different with your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I never had the, the occasion to ask my [SIGHS] grandmom about it. And so I don't know. I know one thing, that they-- when they got married, they were very young. My mom was a good-looking woman. So was my father. But whether, whether they had a good life, I don't know. I never wanted to ask about it. Between my grandparents, if I asked too many things about my mother, they started to cry. She was only 32 years old when she died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always say, to me, it was a Holocaust when I lost my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not with-- when you're three months old. As a matter of fact, when I grew up and got a little-- couple years old, old, I used to call my grandmom, 'Mom,' and my grandfather, 'Father,' because I, I heard mine-- my aunts calling her that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kept telling me, this is not your parents. This is your grandfather and grandmom, which I never could believe that. I always asked, why are they raising me if I'm not their son? Because they love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what-- life went on like that. Later on, I realized that it's not right to call them father and mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always said to my grandmother, if I'll grow up, I'll pay you back what I did-- what you did for me. And the last time when I saw them before they perished, I reminded them. That's why I didn't want to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The right side, I reminded them. I said to-- there, Grandmom, remember, I told you I'll take care of you? I was already in Birkenau when I said it the second time. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] That's how we lived. They kept me nice, clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Not, not a lot to talk about. Like I said, my grandparents were-- they had more feeling to me. But my uncle, my two aunts, they made money, too. But sometimes you just looked away. But I was just more happy to be with them than at my father's place. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When a holiday comes, like Yom Kippur, in Europe, we used to get the kids together. The head of the house used to bless the children. So when it comes-- I was at my grandparents' house. My father-- my grandparents said, why don't you go to your father? You'll get blessed over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got there, they were all blessed. And I was the oddball. I was-- I never had a chance. I was never blessed from none of them. My grandfather couldn't say it in the prayers as a son. And my father didn't wait for me. And I was like a lost soul. But it's OK. I was still happy, always happy. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And that's how life was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. Yes, I went to school, not for too long. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As far as I remember, I think I went through about five grades. That's when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have religious education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much. I went to Talmud Torah. It's like-- it-- like, you had everything in there, from aleph-- from A to Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Secular and religious--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in one place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gomorrah, Russia, everything. Like I mentioned before, my father's prayer was that I would-- should be a rabbi, which-- I wasn't allowed to say I didn't want it or anything. But I didn't like to-- the idea of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to be a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to work for a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbis didn't work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it was a different life for a rabbi in Europe. A, a, a rabbi in Europe was different than here--

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: --than in this country. It was always criticized. They call them names-- like, man takes money for nothing. That's what-- as far as I-- that's what I heard when I grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figure to know about religion is a lot. It means a lot. But I don't have to become a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. My grandparents, my father, they-- my grandfather had a beard, a long beard. My father didn't have a beard. But he didn't shave. He was not-- it's not-- to use-- it's not allowed to use a knife on your face, a razor. So they used a machine for him just to cut his hair-- his beard. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My grandfather used to go to shul three times a day for prayers. Summer or winter, it didn't matter. He-- one thing he observed, very observant for everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, most of the time, yes. Yes. He, he was a [NON-ENGLISH], read the Torah every Saturday. And that's how I grew up. I did that, too. I read the Torah. I knew how to do everything. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have side curls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Payos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, not as curly, the, the way they have them here. Just to-- the barber had to leave me some, not to touch it, just even them up. And that's all. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's how life was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have friends, a lot of Christian friends-- as a matter of fact, mostly Christian friends. They spoke our language, the, the, the boys-- the Christian friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one time, a friend of mine beat up one of the Christian friends. So the Christian friend went to the rabbi-- to the teacher-- to, to claim that so-and-so beat me up. So he says to the Christian guy, he says, what cheder are you going to? He says, I don't go to any cheder. I'm-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his name, Stanley, like. He said, then you're a shegetz? He says, yes, I am a shegetz.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how we lived. We grew up with each other till the war broke out. And they changed their mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you grew up, you didn't know too much about anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, but everything was supposed to be in a joke, where they make fun of a Jew or anything. It wasn't so sharp. It wasn't so that you would feel bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make fun of them back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we never made fun. No, no, the Polish people are very fanatic, very fanatic. If you had anything, sometimes they would say so-and-so-- you'll excuse me, but I want to say that-- they urinated near a cross. See, down there in Poland every few miles, you have a cross, you know, with the blessed mother. And you-- there, you salute. You take your cap off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you did-- they took you to court. Why did you urinate near a cross? It was a lie. Nobody would ever do a thing like that. Just so-- a guy didn't like a Jew, took him to court. And he got about two years jail. So religion wasn't separated from church. When you went to court, if you had to swear, they didn't give you a, a Bible like they give you here. They gave you a New Testament Bible to swear on it, not our Bible. And you had crosses in the-- in the-- in the-- in court because they were not separated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And most of the time, the Jew lost if it went to court.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So once in awhile, they-- one of my friends would put on a-- an armband. That was before the war. He would walk around near the Jewish stores. He would say, don't buy it from Jews. So one time, I said to him, why are you doing that? He says, don't worry. Let them go when I'm not there. They pay me for that, by Christian, Christian stores. But that's how they lived. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friend with the armband, when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war-- '37, '38, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they already turned on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your dreams for yourself? Or what did you think you would do as an adult?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] I was 12, 13 years old. I started to work on my trade, a cabinetmaker, which-- I didn't get paid just to help out, how to handle the trade. And the dream was that I'll grow up as fast as possible, so I can help out, whether it was my father or my grandparents, that I can make money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The dream also was that you go to live in Warsaw. In Warsaw, everybody could make-- anybody could make a living. But not in a province-- little town, like I was born-- I was from.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your town was not far from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Poland wasn't a big country to begin with. You take any kind of little town, they're all near Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: One-- like, what people used to do to better themselves, they went into Germany. Most of the German Jews were foreigners. A lot of them were born there. And it's-- when you went to Germany, you bettered yourself. Like we lived near East Prussia, Konigsberg. That was the-- today, it's no more Konigsberg. It's Kaliningrad. The Russians still have it. They'll never give it back. It's the best part of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, all right, we had a lot of dreams. But it turned out-- turned-- everything turned sour. What did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I belonged to a, a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It's a Zionist organization. The reason I belonged there-- because there-- friends of mine belonged there. They were older than me. They, they-- we-- and so just to kill time, we belonged to that organization. And we had a, a leader. And there was-- there, there was also all kinds of organizations. We had Betar, Bund.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I was too young. We used to buy a little ball and go out in the field on a farm someplace. We used to play. It's like-- we were crazy for it, like in-- baseball in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soccer. We used to play baseball, too, but in a different way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the-- you, you start a, a, a pitcher. We did-- pitched ourself. Yeah, it was a lot of fun. But it wasn't as popular as it is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your kind of family circumstance, did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a bar mitzvah. My father said the-- my father said the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You know, that he's-- that I'm-- in one word, that I'm on my own. Because before the bar mitzvah, any thing-- any things you do wrong it's-- goes on your father's account. When you become bar mitzvah, he's washing himself off from me. So everything is-- I'm on my own. And [LAUGHS] we were too poor to have a party or anything. So we just-- in, in this synagogue, we had a, a Seder after. And that was it. I didn't have to be prepared for it. I knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your haftarah--

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from which parshah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't remember. [LAUGHS] I would not remember. See, what it is with the-- in Europe, a, a, a, a zloty-- that's like a dollar-- it would cost to take out the birth certificate. We couldn't afford it. So sometime-- my brother was going to the army already. He never had the certificate. It was still in, in, in City Hall. We never took it out. We couldn't afford it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So sometimes, if I'm thinking anything, that that's not my real age. I may even cheat on two, two years-- two years younger. But I made up-- I made up the age when I got out of prison camp. I made it up. I was too young to remember my age.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no certificate, nothing. And you-- so went a-- to a-- I had witnesses. And we went to notary public in Germany. We made up. But I know one I thing I did remember-- that I was born on Lag BaOmer. That I remember. But when, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 32nd day-- 33rd day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: --after Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, what, what year, I didn't know. That's after Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, between--

speaker: interviewee
text: Between Passover--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Passover--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and Shavuot.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your friend put on an armband and would go and say, don't buy in Jewish stores. When had things started to take that turn against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: When Hitler came to power.

speaker: interviewee
text: '33, yes. That's when they start building up in Poland. They started to build up groups, anti-Semitic groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the one hand, in your own personal life, there was not much anti-Semitism that was sharp.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there wasn't much, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on the other hand, around you, there were armbands. And what else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] I didn't ever mention it to nobody, to my friends, and never even to my wife. My grandfather used to rent orchards to make a couple hundred dollars-- zlotys, their money. And one time, my grandfather and I were walking. It was way out of town. We walked. And one guy got ahold of my grandfather, took his cap away. He dragged him over to a cross. He wanted him to kiss the cross. Now, my grandfather would get killed. But he wouldn't do it. He beat up my grandfather. I was only a little kid. I jumped on the guy. He just threw me back. I fell on the ground. And finally-- that was going on for about an hour. And my father-- my grandfather wouldn't go away till he gives him back his cap. And finally, I cried. He cried. And he gave it back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This incident happened. There was more than that to happen to other people, too. But that, I-- I, I saw that incident. That happened to father-- my grandfather, rather. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Certain, certain Polish people were getting ready to invite Hitler. They were happy to invite to-- even though he came in, and he ruined the whole country. But they weren't smart enough to realize that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a sense, with your brother going off to the army, that war was coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, we expected it because if you listened to a radio in, in German-- you could listen to the radio, hear Hitler's speeches, and everything. Well, of course, we lived 40 kilometers from Allenstein. That's a German-- already in Germany. And we could hear the hate. The speeches he's-- every speech was in-- much with hate. I understood every word he said in German. This is hoch German. And-- because we had ethnic Germans live with us. They-- by generation, generations, they were Germans. And we lived nicely with them, better with them than with the Poles. Till Hitler came in. Then they changed not just an armband, they changed the whole uniform. Put on a-- right away, they put on the yellow uniform. They became-- they joined the party, the National Socialist Party. And they became Nazis.
